reasoning: The reference value for 2025-10-26 is 4.88%, which acts as our baseline. According to FRED data, this is a local minimum, having trended down from ~5.00% earlier in October. A Reuters poll in mid-October 2025 of 75 bond strategists forecasts the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield—which corporate bond yields closely track—to rise from 4.00% to 4.10% over the next 3-6 months. Experts highlight factors like a rising term premium, steady (sticky) inflation, and immense U.S. debt as the main drivers preventing further drops in long-end yields, even amidst anticipated near-term short-rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. Furthermore, corporate credit spreads are widely reported to be historically tight; therefore, any market volatility or economic shock has asymmetric upside risk for the spreads (pushing yields higher). A slight upward bias is corroborated by FRED's short-term predictive model (estimated P(increase) ~ 0.63 for Nov 2). However, given that bond yields predominantly follow a random walk over the medium-to-long term, forecast confidence naturally decays, thereby necessitating probabilities leaning towards 0.50 as the horizon extends into late November 2025 and January 2026.
